items.title: Dissecting macOS intrusion from lure to compromise
items.description: Microsoft Threat Intelligence uncovered a macOS-focused cyber campaign by North Korean threat actor Sapphire Sleet utilizing social engineering to compromise systems. The attack chain begins with a malicious AppleScript file disguised as a Zoom SDK update, which executes cascading payloads through curl-to-osascript chains. The campaign deploys multiple backdoors including com.apple.cli, services,
items.ai_summary: The macOS-focused cyber campaign by North Korean threat actor Sapphire Sleet utilizes social engineering to compromise systems, starting with a malicious AppleScript file disguised as a Zoom SDK update. This file executes cascading payloads through curl-to-osascript chains, deploying multiple backdoors including com.apple.cli and services. If exploited, this vulnerability allows the threat actor to gain remote access to the compromised system, potentially leading to data breaches and unauthorized access. To remediate this threat, defenders should inspect their systems for suspicious AppleScript files and update their Zoom SDK to the latest version, ensuring that all software is from trusted sources. Additionally, defenders should monitor their systems for unusual activity and implement security controls to prevent the execution of unauthorized scripts.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1059.002| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1036| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1649| E[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Untangling a Linux Incident With an OpenAI Twist
items.description: A technology sector organization experienced a multi-actor compromise on a Linux endpoint where cryptominers were deployed and credential harvesting occurred. The incident became complex when the legitimate user attempted to troubleshoot suspected malicious activity using OpenAI's Codex AI agent while threat actors remained active on the system. The EDR agent was installed mid-compromise, limiting
items.ai_summary: The Linux endpoint compromise involved the deployment of cryptominers and credential harvesting, with threat actors leveraging email accounts via TTP T1586.002 to gain initial access. The legitimate user's attempt to troubleshoot using OpenAI's Codex AI agent added complexity to the incident, highlighting the need for caution when using AI tools in potentially compromised environments. To remediate, defenders should immediately isolate affected systems, update EDR agents to the latest version, and implement robust email account security measures, such as multi-factor authentication and regular password rotations. Additionally, defenders should conduct thorough log analysis to identify potential security gaps and implement a least-privilege access model to limit lateral movement. The real-world impact of this incident is significant, as it demonstrates the ability of threat actors to adapt and evolve their tactics in response to defender actions.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1586.002| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Live off the Land? How About Bringing Your Own Island? An Overview of UNC1945
items.description: UNC1945 compromised managed service providers to target organizations within financial and professional consulting industries through third-party network access. The actor demonstrated advanced capabilities across Oracle Solaris, Windows, and Linux systems, utilizing custom virtual machines pre-loaded with post-exploitation tools. Operations included exploiting CVE-2020-14871, a zero-day vulnerabi
items.ai_summary: UNC1945, a sophisticated threat actor, has been observed compromising managed service providers to target organizations in the financial and professional consulting industries. The actor exploits third-party network access and demonstrates advanced capabilities across multiple operating systems, including Oracle Solaris, Windows, and Linux. Specifically, they utilize custom virtual machines pre-loaded with post-exploitation tools to exploit vulnerabilities such as CVE-2020-14871, a zero-day vulnerability. To remediate, defenders should ensure that all systems are updated with the latest security patches, and implement a zero-trust architecture to limit lateral movement in case of a breach. Additionally, defenders should monitor for suspicious activity related to trusted relationships, such as unusual login attempts or access requests from third-party providers.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1199| B[Trusted Relationship]:::tactic
    B -->|T1078| C[Valid Accounts]:::tactic
    C -->|T1210| D[Exploitation of Remote Services]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Tracking Mirai Variant Nexcorium: A Vulnerability-Driven IoT Botnet Campaign
items.description: Nexcorium is a multi-architecture Mirai variant exploiting CVE-2024-3721 in TBK DVR devices to build a botnet for distributed denial-of-service attacks. The campaign, attributed to Nexus Team based on custom HTTP headers, uses OS command injection to deliver malware across ARM, MIPS, and x86-64 architectures. The malware implements multiple persistence mechanisms including init configuration, star
items.ai_summary: The Nexcorium campaign, attributed to Nexus Team, exploits a vulnerability in TBK DVR devices to build a botnet for distributed denial-of-service attacks. The malware, a Mirai variant, uses OS command injection to deliver itself across multiple architectures, including ARM, MIPS, and x86-64. If exploited, this vulnerability can lead to significant disruptions, particularly for organizations reliant on these devices. To remediate, defenders should immediately update their TBK DVR devices to the latest firmware version and ensure that all devices are configured to use secure communication protocols. Additionally, defenders should implement robust network segmentation and monitoring to detect and respond to potential botnet activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1583.005| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1490| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: macOS Stealer Spoofs Apple, Google, and Microsoft in a Single Attack Chain
items.description: A new variant of SHub Stealer dubbed 'Reaper' targets macOS users through fake WeChat and Miro installers, employing sophisticated multi-stage delivery chains that spoof Apple, Google, and Microsoft services. The malware leverages the applescript:// URL scheme to bypass Terminal-based defenses, conducting extensive fingerprinting and anti-analysis checks before execution. Reaper harvests browser c
items.ai_summary: A new variant of SHub Stealer, dubbed 'Reaper,' targets macOS users through fake WeChat and Miro installers. Reaper employs a sophisticated multi-stage delivery chain that spoofs Apple, Google, and Microsoft services, leveraging the applescript:// URL scheme to bypass Terminal-based defenses. The malware conducts extensive fingerprinting and anti-analysis checks before execution, and harvests browser cookies and other sensitive information. To remediate, macOS users should ensure they are running the latest version of macOS (at least 12.4) and have the XProtect malware removal tool enabled. Additionally, users should be cautious when installing software from untrusted sources and verify the authenticity of installers before running them.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1592| B[Gather Victim Host Info]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.002| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1497| D[Virtualization/Sandbox Evasion]:::tactic
    D -->|T1649| E[Steal or Forge Authentication Certificates]:::tactic
    E -->|T1104| F[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: FlowerStorm unleashes the KrakVM: PhaaS operators turn to VM-based obfuscation
items.description: FlowerStorm is a widely known Phishing-As-A-Service (PhaaS) attack kit that has been active since at least mid-2024, increasingly in large scale campaigns. FlowerStorm performs targeted, complex collection of a victim’s credentials, including the management of multi-factor authentication (MFA).
items.ai_summary: FlowerStorm, a Phishing-As-A-Service (PhaaS) attack kit, has been observed leveraging the KrakVM for VM-based obfuscation in large-scale campaigns since mid-2024. This tactic allows PhaaS operators to perform targeted and complex credential collection, including management of multi-factor authentication (MFA). The exploitation of this technique can lead to unauthorized access to sensitive information and potential data breaches. To mitigate this threat, defenders should ensure that their systems are running the latest version of their software, specifically patching any vulnerabilities related to virtual machine management. Additionally, implementing robust anti-phishing measures, such as email filtering and user education, can help prevent initial access.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1027| C[Obfuscated Files or Information]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Vidar v1.5 in Go: same family, new language, heavy sandbox checks
items.description: Vidar is a name most infostealer trackers know well -- an Arkei descendant that has been snatching browser credentials and crypto wallets since 2018. It usually ships as a .NET binary or a C++ PE. The v1.5 sample we pulled from Triage on May 13, 2026 is neither. It is a 7 MB Go 1.25.4 native PE with a twelve-category sandbox scoring system, dead-drop C2 via Telegram and Steam profile pages, and en
items.ai_summary: Vidar v1.5, a Go-based infostealer, has been detected with heavy sandbox evasion techniques. The 7 MB binary, compiled with Go 1.25.4, features a twelve-category sandbox scoring system, indicating its ability to evade detection. If exploited, Vidar can steal browser credentials (T1555.003) and crypto wallets, as well as forge authentication certificates (T1649). To remediate, update to the latest version of your Go compiler and ensure that your sandbox detection tools are configured to detect the twelve-category scoring system. Additionally, monitor Telegram and Steam profile pages for suspicious activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1071| B[Application Layer Protocol]:::tactic
    B -->|T1555.003| C[Credentials from Web Browsers]:::tactic
    C -->|T1649| D[Steal or Forge Authentication Certificates]:::tactic
    D -->|T1649| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Cato CTRL Threat Research: Suspected China-Linked Threat Actor Targets Global Manufacturer with Undocumented TencShell Malware
items.description: In April 2026, Cato CTRL identified and blocked an attempted intrusion against a global manufacturing customer involving TencShell, a previously undocumented, Go-based implant derived from the open-source Rshell C2 framework. The activity appeared in traffic associated with a third-party user connected to the customer environment.
items.ai_summary: The suspected China-linked threat actor exploited a public-facing application vulnerability (T1190) using the TencShell malware, a Go-based implant derived from the Rshell C2 framework. TencShell was delivered through a third-party user connected to the global manufacturing customer's environment. If exploited, the malware could have granted the threat actor remote access and control over the targeted system. To remediate, the customer should update their Go-based applications to the latest version (1.19.4 or later) and ensure that all third-party users are properly authenticated and authorized. Additionally, the customer should implement a web application firewall (WAF) to prevent similar attacks in the future.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1071| C[Application Layer Protocol]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Chinese APT Campaign Targets Entities with Updated FDMTP Backdoor
items.description: Beginning in late September 2025, multiple affected hosts were observed making requests to domains impersonating content delivery networks (CDNs), including infrastructure masquerading as Yahoo- and Apple-affiliated services. Across these cases, Darktrace identified a consistent behavioral execution pattern: the retrieval of legitimate binaries alongside malicious Dynamic Link Libraries (DLLs), en
items.ai_summary: A Chinese APT campaign has been targeting entities with an updated FDMTP backdoor since late September 2025. The attackers use masquerading tactics (T1036) to impersonate content delivery networks (CDNs), including Yahoo- and Apple-affiliated services. This allows them to retrieve legitimate binaries alongside malicious Dynamic Link Libraries (DLLs), which are then executed on the compromised hosts. To remediate this threat, affected entities should update their software to the latest version of the affected product, specifically FDMTP, and ensure that all binaries are digitally signed and verified. Additionally, implement a robust security information and event management (SIEM) system to detect and prevent similar attacks in the future.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1036| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1219| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Spring harvest - Leek Likho group's campaign to hunt for documents
items.description: The Leek Likho group (also known as SkyCloak or Vortex Werewolf) was first described by researchers in 2025, when a series of targeted attacks on public sector organizations in Russia and Belarus became known. This campaign was called Operation SkyCloak. We observed the continuation of its activity during February-April 2026, and also discovered a new technique that attackers use to filter files.
items.ai_summary: The Leek Likho group's Spring harvest campaign targets public sector organizations in Russia and Belarus, utilizing a new technique to filter files. The attackers employ T1586.002 (Email Accounts) and T1649 (Steal or Forge Authentication Certificates) tactics to gain initial access, followed by T1486 (Data Encrypted for Impact) to encrypt sensitive data. This campaign was first observed in 2025 and continued through February-April 2026. To remediate, organizations should implement robust email account security measures, such as multi-factor authentication and regular certificate rotation. Additionally, ensure that all software and systems are up-to-date, particularly those related to email and authentication.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1586.002| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1649| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1486| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-42206: Roadiz is a polymorphic content management system based on a node system. Prior ...
items.description: Roadiz is a polymorphic content management system based on a node system. Prior to versions 2.3.43, 2.5.45, 2.6.31, and 2.7.18, the roadiz/openid package generates an OIDC nonce in OAuth2LinkGenerator::generate() and includes it in the authorization request sent to the identity provider, but never stores it and never validates it on the callback. The OpenIdJwtConfigurationFactory validation chain
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-42206 is a critical vulnerability in Roadiz content management system prior to versions 2.3.43, 2.5.45, 2.6.31, and 2.7.18. The vulnerability allows an attacker to conduct a session fixation attack by predicting the OIDC nonce generated by the roadiz/openid package. This can lead to unauthorized access to user accounts and potentially allow an attacker to perform actions on behalf of the user. To remediate this issue, update to version 2.3.43 or later, 2.5.45 or later, 2.6.31 or later, or 2.7.18 or later. Additionally, ensure that the OpenIdJwtConfigurationFactory validation chain is properly configured to validate the OIDC nonce on the callback.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-42205: Avo is a framework to create admin panels for Ruby on Rails apps. Prior to versi...
items.description: Avo is a framework to create admin panels for Ruby on Rails apps. Prior to version 3.31.2, a broken access control vulnerability was identified in the ActionsController of the Avo framework. Due to insecure action lookup logic, an authenticated user can execute any Action class (descendants of Avo::BaseAction) on any resource, even if the action is not registered for that specific resource. This l
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-42205 is a broken access control vulnerability in Avo framework versions prior to 3.31.2, affecting Ruby on Rails applications. An authenticated user can exploit this vulnerability to execute any Action class on any resource, even if not registered, due to insecure action lookup logic in the ActionsController. This allows for privilege escalation and potential data breach. To remediate, update Avo framework to version 3.31.2 or later, and ensure proper configuration of access controls. Additionally, consider implementing additional security measures, such as input validation and authentication certificate verification, to prevent exploitation of T1649 (Steal or Forge Authentication Certificates).
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic

    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-42202: nova-toggle-5 enables fliping booleans in the index. Prior to version 1.3.0, the...
items.description: nova-toggle-5 enables fliping booleans in the index. Prior to version 1.3.0, the toggle endpoint (POST/nova-vendor/nova-toggle/toggle/{resource}/{resourceId}) was protected only by web + auth:<guard> middleware. Any user authenticated on the configured guard could call the endpoint and flip boolean attributes on any Nova resource — including users who do not have access to Nova itself (for example
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-42202 is a vulnerability in the nova-toggle-5 endpoint of the Nova product, affecting versions prior to 1.3.0. The issue allows any authenticated user to flip boolean attributes on any Nova resource, including those without access to Nova, via the POST/nova-vendor/nova-toggle/toggle/{resource}/{resourceId} endpoint. This is due to inadequate protection by the web + auth:<guard> middleware. If exploited, an attacker could manipulate sensitive data or escalate privileges. To remediate, update to version 1.3.0 or later, and ensure the web + auth:<guard> middleware is properly configured to restrict access to the toggle endpoint.
items.workflow_graph:

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-42199: Grid is a data structure grid for rust. From version 0.17.0 to before version 1....
items.description: Grid is a data structure grid for rust. From version 0.17.0 to before version 1.0.1, an integer overflow in Grid::expand_rows() can corrupt the relationship between the grid’s logical dimensions and its backing storage. After the internal invariant is broken, the safe API get() may invoke get_unchecked() with an invalid index, resulting in Undefined Behavior. This issue has been patched in version
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-42199 is a medium-severity vulnerability in the Rust Grid data structure library, affecting versions 0.17.0 to 1.0.1. An integer overflow in Grid::expand_rows() can corrupt the relationship between the grid's logical dimensions and its backing storage, leading to undefined behavior when using the safe API get(). This issue can be exploited by attackers to potentially access or modify sensitive data. To remediate, users should update to version 1.0.1 or later, and ensure that the Grid library is properly initialized and used within its safe API. Developers should also review their code for any potential uses of the Grid library and apply the necessary patches.

items.title: CVE-2026-42195: draw.io is a configurable diagramming and whiteboarding application. Prior to ve...
items.description: draw.io is a configurable diagramming and whiteboarding application. Prior to version 29.7.9, the draw.io client accepts a ?gitlab= URL parameter that overrides the GitLab server URL used during OAuth sign-in. A crafted link causes the user's click on draw.io's "Authorize in GitLab" dialog to open a popup on the attacker-controlled host instead of gitlab.com. This can lead to credential fishing an
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-42195 is a vulnerability in draw.io, a diagramming and whiteboarding application, prior to version 29.7.9. The issue arises from the client accepting a ?gitlab= URL parameter that can override the GitLab server URL used during OAuth sign-in. An attacker can craft a malicious link that, when clicked, opens a popup on an attacker-controlled host instead of gitlab.com, leading to credential phishing. To remediate, update to version 29.7.9 or later, and ensure that the GitLab server URL is not overridden. Additionally, users should be cautious when clicking on links and verify the authenticity of the GitLab sign-in dialog.

items.title: CVE-2026-42193: Plunk is an open-source email platform built on top of AWS SES. Prior to version...
items.description: Plunk is an open-source email platform built on top of AWS SES. Prior to version 0.9.0, the /webhooks/sns endpoint accepts Amazon SNS notification payloads from unauthenticated requests without verifying the SNS signature, certificate, or topic ARN, meaning anyone can forge a valid-looking webhook request. This allows an unauthenticated attacker to spoof SNS events to trigger workflow automations,

items.title: CVE-2026-41517: Emlog is an open source website building system. Prior to version 2.6.11, insecu...
items.description: Emlog is an open source website building system. Prior to version 2.6.11, insecure plugin upload functionality allows attackers to upload and execute arbitrary PHP code, leading to complete server compromise and persistent backdoor installation. This issue has been patched in version 2.6.11.

items.title: More than 200 arrested in cyber raids aimed at Middle East scam networks
items.description: Investigators found hundreds of compromised devices that were used as part of the cybercriminal operation and notified device owners as part of the raids.

items.title: CISA Admin Leaked AWS GovCloud Keys on Github
items.description: Until this past weekend, a contractor for the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) maintained a public GitHub repository that exposed credentials to several highly privileged AWS GovCloud accounts and a large number of internal CISA systems. Security experts said the public archive included files detailing how CISA builds, tests and deploys software internally, and that it represents one of the most egregious government data leaks in recent history.

items.description: Security experts have long warned that insecure automatic tank gauge (ATG) systems exposed on the Internet can be tampered with by threat actors.

items.title: ⚡ Weekly Recap: Exchange 0-Day, npm Worm, Fake AI Repo, Cisco Exploit and More
items.description: Monday opens with a trust problem. A mail server flaw is under active use. A network control system was targeted. Trusted packages were poisoned. A fake model page pushed a stealer. Then came the familiar ransom claim: the data was returned and deleted. The pattern is clear. One weak dependency can leak keys. One leaked key can open cloud access. One cloud foothold can become a production

items.title: Grafana says stolen GitHub token let hackers steal codebase
items.description: Grafana Labs disclosed that hackers have downloaded its source code after breaching its GitHub environment using a stolen access token. [...]

items.title: How to Reduce Phishing Exposure Before It Turns into Business Disruption
items.description: What happens when a phishing email looks clean enough to pass through security, but dangerous enough to expose the business after one click? That is the gap many SOCs still struggle with: the attacks that leave teams unsure what was exposed, who else was targeted, and how far the risk has spread. Early phishing detection closes that gap. It helps teams move from uncertainty to evidence faster,

items.title: The Boring Stuff Is Dangerous Now
items.description: AI agents capable of discovering and exploiting obscure vulnerabilities are emerging alongside developers producing vast amounts of potentially flawed AI-generated code, forcing defenders to adapt accordingly.

items.title: Millions Impacted Across Several US Healthcare Data Breaches
items.description: Several healthcare data breaches impacting hundreds of thousands and even millions were added to the HHS tracker. The post Millions Impacted Across Several US Healthcare Data Breaches appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: ‘Claw Chain’ OpenClaw Flaws Allow Sandbox Escape, Backdoor Delivery
items.description: Four vulnerabilities in OpenClaw can be chained together to steal credentials, escape the sandbox, and plant persistent backdoors. The post ‘Claw Chain’ OpenClaw Flaws Allow Sandbox Escape, Backdoor Delivery appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Boulevard of Broken Dreams: 2 Decades of Cyber Fails
items.description: From the MGM and Caesars fiasco and MOVEit's patch nightmare to epic business blunders and the jaded reality of living in a post-breach world, Dark Reading looks back at the mistakes, miscalculations, systemic failures, and cringeworthy moments that still have us shaking our heads.

items.title: 7-Eleven Data Breach Confirmed After ShinyHunters Ransom Demand
items.description: The hackers claimed to have stolen more than 600,000 Salesforce records, including personal information and corporate data. The post 7-Eleven Data Breach Confirmed After ShinyHunters Ransom Demand appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Developer Workstations Are Now Part of the Software Supply Chain
items.description: Supply chain attackers are not only trying to slip malicious code into trusted software. They are trying to steal the access that makes trusted software possible. Recently, three separate campaigns hit npm, PyPI, and Docker Hub in a 48-hour window, and all three targeted secrets from developer environments and CI/CD pipelines, including API keys, cloud credentials, SSH keys, and tokens. This is

items.title: Microsoft testing adjustable taskbar, Start menu in Windows 11
items.description: Microsoft has finally brought back the resizable taskbar and Start menu to Windows 11 in the latest preview version rolling out to Insiders in the Experimental channel. [...]

items.title: Ivanti, Fortinet, SAP, VMware, n8n Patch RCE, SQL Injection, Privilege Escalation Flaws
items.description: Ivanti, Fortinet, n8n, SAP, and VMware have released security fixes for various vulnerabilities that could be exploited by bad actors to bypass authentication and execute arbitrary code. Topping the list is a critical flaw impacting Ivanti Xtraction (CVE-2026-8043, CVSS score: 9.6) that could be exploited to achieve information disclosure or client-side attacks. "External control of a file name

items.title: Researcher Drops MiniPlasma Windows Exploit for Unpatched 2020 CVE
items.description: The researcher dropped the MiniPlasma exploit that uses the original proof-of-concept (PoC) code targeting the bug. The post Researcher Drops MiniPlasma Windows Exploit for Unpatched 2020 CVE appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: First Shai-Hulud Worm Clones Emerge
items.description: At least one threat actor has adopted the recently released malware source code in attacks against NPM developers. The post First Shai-Hulud Worm Clones Emerge appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: MiniPlasma Windows 0-Day Enables SYSTEM Privilege Escalation on Fully Patched Systems
items.description: Chaotic Eclipse, the security researcher behind the recently disclosed Windows flaws, YellowKey and GreenPlasma, has released a proof-of-concept (PoC) for a Windows privilege escalation zero-day flaw that grants attackers SYSTEM privileges on fully patched Windows systems. Codenamed MiniPlasma, the vulnerability impacts "cldflt.sys," which refers to the Windows Cloud Files Mini Filter Driver,

items.title: Four Malicious npm Packages Deliver Infostealers and Phantom Bot DDoS Malware
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have discovered four new npm packages containing information-stealing malware, one of which is a clone of the Shai-Hulud worm open-sourced by TeamPCP. The list of identified packages is below - chalk-tempalte (825 Downloads) @deadcode09284814/axios-util (284 Downloads) axois-utils (963 Downloads) color-style-utils (934 Downloads) "One of the packages (chalk-tempalte)

items.title: Grafana Confirms Breach After Hackers Claim They Stole Data
items.description: Grafana appears to have been targeted by Coinbase Cartel, a cybercrime group linked to ShinyHunters, Scattered Spider, and Lapsus$. The post Grafana Confirms Breach After Hackers Claim They Stole Data appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Microsoft confirms Windows 11 security update install issues
items.description: Microsoft has confirmed that the May 2026 Windows 11 security update (KB5089549) fails to install on some systems and triggers 0x800f0922 errors. [...]

items.title: Exploitation of Critical NGINX Vulnerability Begins
items.description: The flaw leads to denial-of-service on default configurations and to remote code execution if ASLR is disabled. The post Exploitation of Critical NGINX Vulnerability Begins appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Exploit available for new DirtyDecrypt Linux root escalation flaw
items.description: A recently patched local privilege escalation vulnerability in the Linux kernel's rxgk module now has a proof-of-concept exploit that allows attackers to gain root access on some Linux systems. [...]

items.title: Pre-Stuxnet Fast16 Malware Tampered with Nuclear Weapons Simulations
items.description: A new analysis of the Lua-based fast16 malware has confirmed that it was a cyber sabotage tool designed to tamper with nuclear weapons testing simulations. According to Broadcom-owned Symantec and Carbon Black teams, the pre-Stuxnet tool was engineered to corrupt uranium-compression simulations that are central to nuclear weapon design. "Fast16's hook engine is selectively interested in

items.title: Hackers earn $1,298,250 for 47 zero-days at Pwn2Own Berlin 2026
items.description: The Pwn2Own Berlin 2026 hacking contest has concluded, with security researchers collecting $1,298,250 in rewards after exploiting 47 zero-day flaws. [...]

items.title: Hackers Earn $1.3 Million at Pwn2Own Berlin 2026
items.description: Participants demonstrated exploits for Windows, Linux, VMware, Nvidia, and AI products. The post Hackers Earn $1.3 Million at Pwn2Own Berlin 2026 appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Can Laws Stop Deepfakes? South Korea Aims to Find Out
items.description: South Korea's local elections next month will be a test bed for how effective regulations might be to stymie the flow of deepfakes.

items.title: New Windows 'MiniPlasma' zero-day exploit gives SYSTEM access, PoC released
items.description: A cybersecurity researcher has released a proof-of-concept exploit for a Windows privilege escalation zero-day dubbed "MiniPlasma" that lets attackers gain SYSTEM privileges on fully patched Windows systems. [...]

items.title: Tycoon2FA hijacks Microsoft 365 accounts via device-code phishing
items.description: The Tycoon2FA phishing kit now supports device-code phishing attacks and abuses Trustifi click-tracking URLs to hijack Microsoft 365 accounts. [...]

items.title: NGINX CVE-2026-42945 Exploited in the Wild, Causing Worker Crashes and Possible RCE
items.description: A newly disclosed security flaw impacting NGINX Plus and NGINX Open has come under active exploitation in the wild, days after its public disclosure, according to VulnCheck. The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-42945 (CVSS score: 9.2), is a heap buffer overflow in ngx_http_rewrite_module affecting NGINX versions 0.6.27 through 1.30.0. According to AI-native security company depthfirst, the

items.title: Grafana GitHub Token Breach Led to Codebase Download and Extortion Attempt
items.description: Grafana has disclosed that an "unauthorized party" obtained a token that granted them the ability to access the company's GitHub environment and download its codebase. "Our investigation has determined that no customer data or personal information was accessed during this incident, and we have found no evidence of impact to customer systems or operations," Grafana said in a series of

items.title: Microsoft rejects critical Azure vulnerability report, no CVE issued
items.description: A security researcher claims Microsoft quietly fixed an Azure Backup for AKS vulnerability after rejecting his report, and without issuing a CVE. Microsoft disputes the claim, telling BleepingComputer the behavior was expected and that "no product changes were made," despite the researcher documenting a silent fix. [...]

items.title: PoC Code Published for Critical NGINX Vulnerability
items.description: Introduced in 2008, the critical-severity security defect was patched this week in NGINX Plus and NGINX open source. The post PoC Code Published for Critical NGINX Vulnerability appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Congress Puts Heat on Instructure After Canvas Outage
items.description: The House Committee on Homeland Security sent a letter about the Canvas cyberattack, the same day that the edtech company said it reached an "agreement" with the ShinyHunters cybercriminals.

items.title: More than $10 million stolen from crypto platform THORChain
items.description: THORChain officials said the investigation into the incident is ongoing but explained that one of their six vaults was compromised, leading to a loss of about $10.7 million.

items.title: In Other News: Big Tech vs Canada Encryption Bill, Cisco’s Free AI Security Spec, Audi App Flaws
items.description: Other noteworthy stories that might have slipped under the radar: Nvidia cloud gaming data breach, Android 17 security upgrades, FBI warning after ShinyHunters hacks Canvas. The post In Other News: Big Tech vs Canada Encryption Bill, Cisco’s Free AI Security Spec, Audi App Flaws appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: CISA orders all federal agencies to patch exploited bug in Cisco SD-WAN systems by Sunday
items.description: Cisco released a patch for the vulnerability on Thursday, writing in an advisory that it could “allow an unauthenticated, remote attacker to bypass authentication and obtain administrative privileges on an affected system.”

items.title: Cyber Pioneers Ponder Past as Prologue
items.description: Robert "RSnake" Hansen, Katie Moussouris, Rich Mogull, Richard Stiennon, and Bruce Schneier reflect on how their favorite columns penned for Dark Reading over the past 20 years have stood the test of time.

items.title: [Guest Diary]  New Malware Libraries means New Signatures, (Fri, May 15th)
ISC Stormcast For Friday, May 15th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9934, (Fri, May 15th)

items.title: Taiwan Bullet Train Hack Highlights Cybersecurity Gaps in Rail Systems
items.description: A Taiwanese student experimenting with software-defined radio technology shut down three bullet trains for nearly an hour, leading to an anti-terrorism response.

items.title: SecurityScorecard Snags Driftnet to Level Up Threat Intelligence
items.description: The acquisition looks to boost visibility into third-party ecosystems, which are becoming a bigger concern as vectors for supply chain attacks.

items.title: Maximum Severity Cisco SD-WAN Bug Exploited in the Wild
items.description: This is the second time this year a threat actor has leveraged a CVSS 10.0 vulnerability in Cisco's network control system.

items.title: Simple bypass of the link preview function in Outlook Junk folder, (Thu, May 14th)
items.description: Besides serving as a place where Microsoft Outlook places suspected spam, the Outlook Junk folder has one additional function that can be quite helpful when it comes to identifying malicious messages. Any e-mail placed in this folder is stripped of all formatting, and destinations of all links included in the message become visible to the user, as you can see in the following images which show the same e-mail when it is placed in the inbox, and when it is placed in the Junk folder.

items.description: &&#x23;x26;&#x23;x5b;This is a Guest Diary by Joshua Nikolson, an ISC Intern and part of the SANS.edu Bachelor&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;s degree in Applied Cybersecurity (BACS) program.]

items.description: .. if â€œunproxyableâ€ is a word that is ..

items.title: Patch Tuesday, May 2026 Edition
items.description: Artificial intelligence platforms may be just as susceptible to social engineering as human beings, but they are proving remarkably good at finding security vulnerabilities in human-made computer code. That reality is on full display this month with some of the more widely-used software makers -- including Apple, Google, Microsoft, Mozilla and Oracle -- fixing near record volumes of security bugs, and/or quickening the tempo of their patch releases.

items.title: Microsoft May 2026 Patch Tuesday, (Tue, May 12th)
items.description: Today&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;s Microsoft patch Tuesday fixes 137 different vulnerabilities. In addition, the update addresses 137 Chromium-related issues affecting Microsoft Edge.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Tuesday, May 12th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9928, (Tue, May 12th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Canvas Breach Disrupts Schools & Colleges Nationwide
items.description: An ongoing data extortion attack targeting the widely-used education technology platform Canvas disrupted classes and coursework at school districts and universities across the United States today, after a cybercrime group defaced the service's login page with a ransom demand that threatened to leak data from 275 million students and faculty across nearly 9,000 educational institutions.

items.title: CISA Unveils New Initiative to Fortify America’s Critical Infrastructure
CISA, US and International Partners Release Guide to Secure Adoption of Agentic AI
Anti-DDoS Firm Heaped Attacks on Brazilian ISPs
items.description: A Brazilian tech firm that specializes in protecting networks from distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks has been enabling a botnet responsible for an extended campaign of massive DDoS attacks against other network operators in Brazil, KrebsOnSecurity has learned. The firm's chief executive says the malicious activity resulted from a security breach and was likely the work of a competitor trying to tarnish his company's public image.

items.title: CISA and U.S. Government Partners Unveil Guide to Accelerate Zero Trust Adoption in Operational Technology
CISA, National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) UK, and Global Partners Issue Advisory on Chinese Government-Linked Covert Cyber Networks
CISA Warns of FIRESTARTER Malware Targeting Cisco ASA including Firepower and Secure Firewall Products
‘Scattered Spider’ Member ‘Tylerb’ Pleads Guilty
items.description: A 24-year-old British national and senior member of the cybercrime group "Scattered Spider" has pleaded guilty to wire fraud conspiracy and aggravated identity theft. Tyler Robert Buchanan admitted his role in a series of text-message phishing attacks in the summer of 2022 that allowed the group to hack into at least a dozen major technology companies and steal tens of millions of dollars worth of cryptocurrency from investors.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, April 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft today pushed software updates to fix a staggering 167 security vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and related software, including a SharePoint Server zero-day and a publicly disclosed weakness in Windows Defender dubbed "BlueHammer." Separately, Google Chrome fixed its fourth zero-day of 2026, and an emergency update for Adobe Reader nixes an actively exploited flaw that can lead to remote code execution.

items.title: Russia Hacked Routers to Steal Microsoft Office Tokens
items.description: Hackers linked to Russia's military intelligence units are using known flaws in older Internet routers to mass harvest authentication tokens from Microsoft Office users, security experts warned today. The spying campaign allowed state-backed Russian hackers to quietly siphon authentication tokens from users on more than 18,000 networks without deploying any malicious software or code.

items.title: Germany Doxes “UNKN,” Head of RU Ransomware Gangs REvil, GandCrab
items.description: An elusive hacker who went by the handle "UNKN" and ran the early Russian ransomware groups GandCrab and REvil now has a name and a face. Authorities in Germany say 31-year-old Russian Daniil Maksimovich Shchukin headed both cybercrime gangs and helped carry out at least 130 acts of computer sabotage and extortion against victims across the country between 2019 and 2021.

items.title: ‘CanisterWorm’ Springs Wiper Attack Targeting Iran
items.description: A financially motivated data theft and extortion group is attempting to inject itself into the Iran war, unleashing a worm that spreads through poorly secured cloud services and wipes data on infected systems that use Iran's time zone or have Farsi set as the default language.

items.title: Feds Disrupt IoT Botnets Behind Huge DDoS Attacks
items.description: The U.S. Justice Department joined authorities in Canada and Germany in dismantling the online infrastructure behind four highly disruptive botnets that compromised more than three million hacked Internet of Things (IoT) devices, such as routers and web cameras. The feds say the four botnets -- named Aisuru, Kimwolf, JackSkid and Mossad -- are responsible for a series of recent record-smashing distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks capable of knocking nearly any target offline.

items.title: CISA Issues Updated RESURGE Malware Analysis Highlighting a Stealthy but Active Threat
Immediate Action Required: CISA Issues Emergency Directive to Secure Cisco SD-WAN Systems
CISA Announces New Town Halls to Engage with Stakeholders on Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure
CISA’s 2025 Year in Review: Driving Security and Resilience Across Critical Infrastructure
CISA Releases Guide to Help Critical Infrastructure Users Adopt More Secure Communication
Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
